title: Economic equality and the role of taxation
summary: Why do market economies produce unequal incomes, how do we measure that inequality, and what can governments do about it? This lesson separates equity from equality, builds the Lorenz curve and Gini coefficient as measuring tools, examines poverty and the poverty cycle, and works through the tax system — direct and indirect, progressive, proportional and regressive — plus transfer payments and other redistribution policies. It closes on the equity–efficiency trade-off and a fully marked Paper 1 part (b) evaluation.
learningObjectives: Distinguish clearly between equity (fairness) and equality (sameness) in the distribution of income.
Construct, read and interpret a Lorenz curve, and calculate and interpret the Gini coefficient.
Explain absolute and relative poverty, their causes, and the mechanics of the poverty cycle.
Classify taxes as direct or indirect and as progressive, proportional or regressive, and explain how each affects the distribution of income.
Evaluate taxation, transfer payments and other redistribution policies, weighing the equity–efficiency trade-off and reaching a reasoned judgement.
levelNote: Common to SL and HL at this level.

simpleExplanation.title: Fair Shares, Not Equal Shares
simpleExplanation.summary: Markets reward people very unequally. Economists measure how unequal a country is, ask whether that is fair, and study the tools — mainly taxes and benefits — a government can use to close the gap without doing so much damage to incentives that the whole economy suffers.
simpleExplanation.analogy: Picture a country's yearly income as a single cake. If everyone got an identical slice that would be perfect EQUALITY. But some people worked night shifts, some took huge risks starting firms, some inherited a bakery — so a fair (EQUITABLE) cut is not the same as an identical cut. The Lorenz curve draws exactly how lopsided the slicing is; the Gini coefficient turns that picture into one number; and taxes and benefits are how the government trims the biggest slices to top up the smallest — mindful that if it trims too hard, fewer people will bother baking.
steps: Separate the two ideas: equality means identical shares; equity means fair shares — they are not the same thing.
Measure the current distribution with the Lorenz curve, then summarise it in one number, the Gini coefficient (0 = perfect equality, 1 = perfect inequality).
Diagnose poverty (absolute vs relative), its causes, and the self-reinforcing poverty cycle.
Apply the tools — progressive taxes and transfer payments pull the Lorenz curve towards the 45° line — while weighing the equity–efficiency trade-off.

flashcards.back: Equality = everyone gets the SAME (identical outcomes or opportunities). Equity = FAIRNESS, which usually means treating people according to need or contribution and can justify UNEQUAL outcomes. A society can be more equitable without being more equal — and vice versa.

A graph plotting the cumulative % of total income (y-axis) against the cumulative % of the population ranked poorest-to-richest (x-axis). The further it bows below the 45° line of perfect equality, the greater the income inequality.
The 45° diagonal from (0,0) to (100,100). On it, the poorest 20% of people earn 20% of income, the poorest 50% earn 50%, and so on — every share matches. No real economy sits on it.

A single number for inequality from 0 (perfect equality) to 1 (perfect inequality), equal to Area A / (Area A + Area B), where A is the gap between the 45° line and the Lorenz curve and B is the area beneath the Lorenz curve. Higher = more unequal.

Absolute poverty = income below a fixed subsistence line (e.g. the international $-a-day line) — inability to meet basic needs. Relative poverty = income far below the typical income of that society (e.g. below 60% of median). A rich country can have relative poverty with almost no absolute poverty.

Low income → low saving → little investment in education, health or capital → low productivity → low income again. The cycle is self-reinforcing across a lifetime and generations, which is why targeted intervention is needed to break it.

A DIRECT tax is levied on income or wealth and paid straight to the government by the person/firm who bears it (income tax, corporation tax, wealth tax). An INDIRECT tax is levied on spending and collected via a third party — the seller (VAT/GST, excise duties).
The AVERAGE tax rate RISES as income rises, so higher earners pay a larger PROPORTION of income in tax. Most reduces inequality. Typical example: income tax with rising marginal bands.

The AVERAGE tax rate FALLS as income rises, so the poor pay a larger PROPORTION of their income. Indirect taxes such as VAT are regressive because low-income households spend a bigger share of income on taxed consumption. Increases inequality.
The average tax rate is CONSTANT at every income level — everyone pays the same percentage. On its own it leaves the Gini coefficient unchanged.

Government payments to individuals for which no good or service is received in return — unemployment benefit, state pensions, child benefit, disability support. They raise the incomes of the poorest, shifting the post-transfer Lorenz curve towards the 45° line.

Redistribution that improves fairness (equity) can blunt incentives to work, save, invest and take risks, potentially shrinking the total 'pie' (efficiency). The policy question is how much equity is worth how much efficiency.

flashcards.front: Exam Trap: progressive ≠ 'higher earners pay more money'
flashcards.back: Almost every tax takes more CASH from the rich. What makes a tax progressive is that it takes a higher PERCENTAGE of income as income rises. Always argue from the average tax rate / proportion of income, never the absolute amount.

A Gini of 0 is perfect EQUALITY, not equity, and is NOT the policy goal. A FALL in the Gini (e.g. 0.42 → 0.35) means inequality has DECREASED. Never say a country is 'more equal' because its Gini is higher.

quickCheck.prompt: A government gives larger housing support to families with disabled children than to families without. This policy is best described as promoting:
options: Equality, because everyone is treated identically
Equity, because support is matched to need even though outcomes differ
Regressive taxation
A fall in the Gini coefficient to zero
quickCheck.answer: Equity, because support is matched to need even though outcomes differ

quickCheck.prompt: Country X's Gini coefficient falls from 0.45 to 0.38 after a reform. This means:
options: Income inequality has increased
Income inequality has decreased
The country has reached perfect equality
Average incomes have fallen

quickCheck.prompt: A tax takes 30% of a low earner's income and 30% of a high earner's income. This tax is:
options: Progressive
Regressive
Proportional
Indirect by definition

quickCheck.prompt: Why is VAT (a flat rate on spending) usually described as regressive?
options: The rich pay more money in total VAT
Low-income households spend a larger PROPORTION of their income on taxed goods
It is collected directly from income
The tax rate rises with income
quickCheck.answer: Low-income households spend a larger PROPORTION of their income on taxed goods

quickCheck.prompt: On a Lorenz curve diagram, a curve that has moved CLOSER to the 45° line of perfect equality indicates:
options: A rise in the Gini coefficient and more inequality
A fall in the Gini coefficient and less inequality
That the tax system has become regressive
That the economy has entered a recession
quickCheck.answer: A fall in the Gini coefficient and less inequality

faq.q: Is the aim of policy to reach a Gini coefficient of 0?
faq.a: No. A Gini of 0 is perfect equality — identical incomes for everyone regardless of effort, skill or risk. Almost all economists reject that as a goal because incentives to work, train, save and start businesses would collapse, shrinking total output. Policy targets equity (fairness) and a reduction in poverty, not the elimination of all inequality. The real debate is about the OPTIMAL level of inequality, not zero.

faq.q: What is the difference between income inequality and wealth inequality?
faq.a: Income is a FLOW earned over a period (a salary per year); wealth is a STOCK of assets held at a point in time (property, shares, savings). Wealth inequality is almost always far larger than income inequality, because assets accumulate over a lifetime and across generations and generate further returns. A country can reduce income inequality through taxes and benefits while wealth inequality keeps widening.

faq.q: If progressive taxes reduce inequality, why not just raise top rates sharply?
faq.a: Because of the equity–efficiency trade-off. Very high marginal rates can weaken the incentive to work extra hours, train, save or take entrepreneurial risk, and can encourage tax avoidance, evasion or emigration of high earners — so revenue may rise by less than expected (or even fall) while the economy grows more slowly. This is why redistribution usually combines moderate progressive taxes with transfers, education and health spending rather than relying on punitive top rates alone.

faq.q: How do transfer payments change the Lorenz curve?
faq.a: Transfers such as pensions and unemployment benefit add to the incomes of the poorest groups. Comparing the distribution BEFORE and AFTER transfers, the post-transfer Lorenz curve lies closer to the 45° line of perfect equality and the Gini coefficient is lower — the standard way exam questions ask you to show that redistribution has reduced measured inequality.

sections.content: Free markets are efficient at generating income, but they distribute it very unequally: rewards flow to those who own scarce resources, valuable skills or capital. Whether that outcome is acceptable is one of the oldest questions in economics. This lesson gives you the vocabulary to describe inequality precisely (equity versus equality), the tools to measure it (the Lorenz curve and Gini coefficient), a framework for poverty, and a full account of the policies — taxation, transfers and more — governments use to redistribute income, always weighed against the cost to efficiency.
Equity is not the same as equality

Students lose marks every year by treating these words as synonyms. EQUALITY means sameness: an equal distribution gives everyone identical shares. EQUITY means fairness, and fairness usually requires treating different people differently — more help for those with greater need, or reward in proportion to effort and contribution. A society that pays a surgeon more than a trainee is unequal, yet most people would call it equitable. A benefit that is larger for a disabled claimant than a healthy one is unequal but widely seen as fair. So a policy can raise equity without raising equality, and forcing perfect equality can even feel inequitable.

items: **Equality** = identical outcomes (or identical opportunities). It is a factual, measurable idea.
**Equity** = fairness. It is a NORMATIVE (value-based) idea, so people reasonably disagree about it.
**Horizontal equity** = treating people in the same situation the same way. **Vertical equity** = treating people in different situations differently (e.g. taxing higher earners at higher rates).
The goal of redistribution policy is usually greater EQUITY and less poverty — not perfect equality.

sections.content: Measuring inequality: the Lorenz curve

To compare countries or track a country over time we need to measure the distribution of income. The Lorenz curve is the standard picture. Rank the whole population from poorest to richest and plot, on the horizontal axis, the cumulative percentage of the population and, on the vertical axis, the cumulative percentage of total income they receive. The 45° diagonal is the LINE OF PERFECT EQUALITY: on it the poorest 20% of people earn exactly 20% of income, the poorest 60% earn 60%, and so on. The actual Lorenz curve always lies below this line and bows away from it — the poorest 20% might earn only 5% of income. The more the curve sags towards the bottom-right corner, the more unequal the distribution.

items: **X-axis:** cumulative % of population, ranked poorest to richest.
**Y-axis:** cumulative % of total income.
**Line of perfect equality:** the 45° line where each population share earns an identical income share.
**Area A** (between the 45° line and the curve) captures the amount of inequality; **Area B** is the area beneath the curve.
A curve that shifts TOWARDS the 45° line means LESS inequality; a curve that bows further away means MORE inequality.

sections.content: The Lorenz curve is powerful because it lets you compare distributions visually and show the effect of policy: draw the pre-tax-and-transfer curve, then the post-tax-and-transfer curve nearer the 45° line, and you have illustrated redistribution in one diagram.

sections.question:
Reading a Lorenz curve. The table gives the income distribution of the country of Meridia.

| Cumulative % of population (poorest first) | Cumulative % of income |
|---|---|
| 0 | 0 |
| 20 | 5 |
| 40 | 14 |
| 60 | 28 |
| 80 | 50 |
| 100 | 100 |

(a) On the line of perfect equality, what share of income would the poorest 40% receive? [1]
(b) What share do the poorest 40% ACTUALLY receive in Meridia, and what does the gap tell you? [2]
(c) The richest 20% of the population — what share of total income do they take? Show your working. [2]
sections.solution:
**(a)** On the 45° line of perfect equality every population share earns an identical income share, so the poorest 40% of people would receive **40% of income**. [1]

**(b)** From the table the poorest 40% actually receive only **14% of income**. [1] The 26-percentage-point gap below the equality benchmark (40% − 14%) shows that income is distributed unequally — the bottom of the distribution receives far less than a proportional share, so Meridia's Lorenz curve bows well below the 45° line. [1]

**(c)** The cumulative income of the poorest 80% is 50%. The remaining income goes to the richest 20%: 100% − 50% = **50% of total income**. [1 for method: taking 100 minus the cumulative share at the 80th percentile.] So the top fifth of the population earns half of all income — the same amount as the other four-fifths combined, confirming pronounced inequality. [1]

sections.content: Turning the picture into a number: the Gini coefficient

A curve is hard to rank in a league table, so we summarise it in one number: the Gini coefficient. It measures how much of the area beneath the 45° line falls in the 'inequality gap' (Area A). It runs from 0 (the curve sits on the 45° line — perfect equality) to 1 (the curve hugs the axes — one person has all the income). Most countries lie between about 0.25 and 0.65. A higher Gini means more inequality; a fall in the Gini over time means the distribution has become more equal.

Gini coefficient = $\dfrac{\text{Area A}}{\text{Area A} + \text{Area B}}$

Area A = the region between the line of perfect equality and the Lorenz curve. Area B = the region beneath the Lorenz curve. Because (Area A + Area B) is the whole triangle under the 45° line, the Gini is simply the fraction of that triangle taken up by the inequality gap.

The Gini coefficient and the Lorenz curve are two views of the SAME information. The Gini rises when the Lorenz curve bows further from the 45° line, and falls when it moves towards it. In an evaluation, use both together: cite the Gini for precision and describe the Lorenz-curve shift for the mechanism. Never claim a country is 'more equal' because its Gini is HIGHER — that is backwards, and it is one of the most common errors examiners see.
Poverty: absolute, relative, and the cycle

Inequality is about the SHAPE of the distribution; poverty is about the BOTTOM of it. Economists distinguish two kinds. ABSOLUTE poverty is an inability to afford the basic necessities of life — food, clean water, shelter, basic health care — measured against a fixed subsistence line (such as the international extreme-poverty line). RELATIVE poverty is being far worse off than the typical person in your own society, usually defined as living below a set fraction (commonly 60%) of median income. A wealthy country may have almost no absolute poverty yet substantial relative poverty; as a country grows richer, absolute poverty tends to fall but relative poverty can persist because the median rises too.

items: **Causes of poverty:** low or no wages (unemployment, low-skill or insecure work), lack of human capital (poor education/health), discrimination, geography, disability or age, and inherited disadvantage.

**The poverty cycle (trap):** low income → low or zero saving → little spending on education, health and capital → low productivity → low income again. The loop repeats within a lifetime and across generations.
Because the cycle is self-reinforcing, markets alone rarely break it — this is a key justification for government INTERVENTION targeted at the poorest.
**Consequences of poverty and high inequality:** lower economic well-being, poorer health and education outcomes, reduced social mobility, and possible social and political instability.

sections.content: The tax system: direct vs indirect, and how the burden falls

Taxes are classified in two independent ways. First, by WHAT they are levied on. DIRECT taxes fall on income or wealth and are paid straight to the government by the person or firm who bears them — income tax, corporation tax, capital-gains and wealth taxes. INDIRECT taxes fall on spending and are collected through a third party, the seller, who passes them to the government — VAT/GST and excise duties on fuel, alcohol and tobacco. Second, and separately, by HOW the burden changes with income — measured by the AVERAGE tax rate (tax paid ÷ income).

items: **Progressive tax:** average tax rate RISES with income — higher earners pay a larger share of income. This is the tax structure that reduces inequality and pulls the Lorenz curve towards the 45° line. Typical example: income tax with rising marginal bands.
**Proportional (flat) tax:** average tax rate is CONSTANT — the same percentage at every income. On its own it leaves relative shares, and so the Gini, unchanged.
**Regressive tax:** average tax rate FALLS as income rises — the poor pay a larger share of their income. Indirect taxes such as VAT are regressive in effect. This structure WIDENS inequality.
Direct/indirect and progressive/regressive are separate axes: income tax is direct and (usually) progressive; VAT is indirect and regressive.

sections.content: You must be able to explain WHY a flat-rate indirect tax like VAT is regressive — the marker is testing whether you reason in PROPORTIONS. Suppose a £120 item carries £20 of VAT. For someone earning £200 a week that £20 is 10% of income; for someone earning £2,000 a week it is 1%. The cash is identical, but the poorer person surrenders a far larger PROPORTION of income, because lower-income households spend a bigger share of what they earn (and save less). Always argue from proportion of income, never absolute amount.

sections.question:
A progressive-tax calculation. Highland uses a two-band income tax:
- 0% on income up to $15,000
- 25% on every dollar of income above $15,000

Person A earns $30,000. Person B earns $90,000.
(a) Calculate the total tax paid and the average tax rate (ATR) for each person. [4]
(b) Use your ATRs to prove the tax is progressive. [1]
(c) Explain, using the idea of the Lorenz curve, how this tax affects income inequality. [3]
sections.solution:
**(a) Person A (income $30,000)**
- Taxable income above the threshold: $30,000 − $15,000 = $15,000
- Total tax: 0.25 × $15,000 = **$3,750**
- ATR: ($3,750 ÷ $30,000) × 100 = **12.5%**

**Person B (income $90,000)**
- Taxable income above the threshold: $90,000 − $15,000 = $75,000
- Total tax: 0.25 × $75,000 = **$18,750**
- ATR: ($18,750 ÷ $90,000) × 100 = **20.83%**

*Marks: [1] A's tax, [1] A's ATR, [1] B's tax, [1] B's ATR.*

**(b)** As income rises from $30,000 to $90,000, the average tax rate rises from 12.5% to 20.83%. Because the ATR INCREASES with income, the tax is **progressive**. [1] (Note: it is the rising average RATE that proves progressivity, not the fact that B pays more dollars.)

**(c)** Progressive income tax takes a larger proportion of income from higher earners and a smaller proportion from lower earners, so it narrows the gap between disposable (post-tax) incomes. On a Lorenz-curve diagram, the post-tax curve lies CLOSER to the 45° line of perfect equality than the pre-tax curve [1], which corresponds to a LOWER Gini coefficient [1]. The tax therefore reduces measured income inequality — though how much depends on the rates chosen and on whether high earners avoid or evade the tax. [1]

sections.content: Transfer payments and other redistribution policies

Taxation is only one side of redistribution — it determines what is taken. What is given back matters just as much. TRANSFER PAYMENTS are government payments to individuals for which no good or service is provided in return: unemployment benefits, state pensions, child benefit, disability and housing support. They raise the disposable incomes of the poorest directly, so the post-transfer Lorenz curve moves towards the 45° line. Governments combine transfers with other tools.

items: **Transfer payments** — cash benefits (pensions, unemployment and disability benefit, child support) that top up low incomes directly.

**Government-provided goods and services** — 'in-kind' benefits such as state education and health care, which raise real living standards and, over time, human capital, helping to break the poverty cycle.
**Minimum wages and labour-market policy** — raise the incomes of the low-paid (though with possible employment effects covered elsewhere).
**Investment in human capital and infrastructure** — education, training and health spending targeted at the poor tackle the ROOT CAUSES of low income rather than only its symptoms.
A credible redistribution strategy usually blends progressive taxes (to fund it) with transfers AND longer-term investment in opportunity.

sections.content: The equity–efficiency trade-off

Redistribution is not free. Taking income from high earners and topping up low earners can improve equity and reduce poverty, but it can also blunt incentives: high marginal tax rates may discourage extra work, saving, investment and entrepreneurial risk, while generous benefits can, if poorly designed, weaken the incentive to seek work (the 'benefits trap'). If these effects shrink total output, the economy has a smaller pie to share — the classic EQUITY–EFFICIENCY TRADE-OFF. The size of the trade-off is debated and empirical: moderate redistribution that funds education and health can even RAISE efficiency by making the workforce more productive and by reducing the waste of talent trapped in poverty. The policy craft lies in choosing tools that buy the most equity for the least loss of efficiency.

items: **The case for redistribution:** reduces poverty and relative deprivation, improves overall economic well-being, can raise long-run productivity via health and education, and supports social and political stability.

**The case against (or for caution):** disincentive effects on work, saving and investment; tax avoidance, evasion and capital/labour flight; administrative cost; and the risk of dependency if benefits are badly designed.
**Judgement:** the question is rarely 'redistribute or not' but 'how, how much, and with which mix of tools' — balancing equity gains against efficiency costs.

sections.content: Common mistakes examiners penalise

items: **Treating equity and equality as the same word** — equality is sameness (measurable); equity is fairness (normative). A fair policy is often deliberately unequal. Muddling them is an instant marker for a weak answer.

**Defining progressive/regressive by the amount of cash paid** — the rich pay more money under almost every tax. Progressivity is about the PROPORTION of income (the average tax rate), so always argue from percentages, not totals.
**Reading the Gini backwards** — a HIGHER Gini means MORE inequality, and a fall in the Gini means the distribution has become more equal. Never say a rising Gini shows a fairer society.
**Claiming a Gini of 0 is the goal** — perfect equality destroys incentives; policy targets equity and lower poverty, not zero inequality.

**Confusing absolute with relative poverty** — absolute poverty is a fixed subsistence line; relative poverty is measured against the society's median. Growth can cut absolute poverty while relative poverty persists.

**One-sided evaluation** — asserting progressive tax 'reduces inequality' with no mention of disincentives, avoidance/evasion, whether it treats root causes, or the need for complementary policies. Part (b) marks REQUIRE both sides and a reasoned judgement.
**A diagram drawn but not explained** — an unlabelled or unexplained Lorenz-curve/tax diagram cannot reach the top band. Reference the axes and the shift in words.

sections.content: Model answer — marked against the real IB markband

sections.question: Paper 1, part (b): Evaluate the effectiveness of progressive taxation as a policy to reduce income inequality. [15 marks]
sections.solution:
**Model answer:**

Income inequality refers to the unequal distribution of income across a population, commonly measured by the Lorenz curve and summarised in the Gini coefficient (0 = perfect equality, 1 = perfect inequality). Progressive taxation is a tax structure in which the AVERAGE tax rate rises as income rises, so higher earners surrender a larger PROPORTION of their income than lower earners. This essay evaluates how effectively such a tax reduces measured inequality.

**How it works (theory + diagram).** By taking a greater share of income from the rich and a smaller share from the poor, progressive income tax compresses the distribution of DISPOSABLE (post-tax) income. *[Diagram: a Lorenz-curve diagram with cumulative % of population on the x-axis, cumulative % of income on the y-axis, the 45° line of perfect equality, an outer pre-tax Lorenz curve, and an inner post-tax Lorenz curve lying CLOSER to the 45° line.]* The inward shift of the curve towards the line of perfect equality represents a FALL in the Gini coefficient — the standard way to show that inequality has been reduced. The revenue raised can also fund transfer payments and public services that lift the incomes and human capital of the poorest, reinforcing the effect.

**Real-world example (developed).** Many high-income economies operate multi-band income taxes — for instance a system taxing income at 0% up to a personal allowance, then at rising marginal rates (say 20%, 40% and 45%) on higher bands. Official statistics in such countries typically show the Gini coefficient for income AFTER taxes and transfers several points below the Gini for market (pre-tax) income — direct evidence that the progressive system, together with the benefits it funds, narrows the distribution. This shows the policy can and does reduce measured inequality in practice.

**Strengths.** (1) It targets inequality directly and is administratively established. (2) It is built on the ability-to-pay principle, so it is widely seen as equitable (vertical equity). (3) The revenue funds transfers, education and health that attack the poverty cycle at its roots, so effects compound over time. (4) It automatically stabilises the distribution as incomes change.

**Limitations (the other side).** (1) DISINCENTIVE EFFECTS: high marginal rates may discourage extra work, saving, investment and entrepreneurial risk-taking — the equity–efficiency trade-off — potentially shrinking the very tax base the policy relies on. (2) TAX AVOIDANCE AND EVASION: high earners have the most resources to exploit loopholes, shift income offshore, or relocate, so headline rates overstate what is actually collected and the Gini falls by less than intended. (3) ROOT CAUSES: progressive tax redistributes the SYMPTOM (unequal income) but does little about the CAUSES — poor education, ill health, discrimination, lack of opportunity — so on its own it does not break the poverty cycle. (4) It touches income but not WEALTH, which is typically far more unequally held. (5) It needs COMPLEMENTARY POLICIES — transfer payments, minimum wages, and above all investment in human capital — to be effective; used alone its impact is limited.

**Reasoned, balanced judgement.** Progressive taxation IS an effective tool for reducing income inequality — the evidence that post-tax-and-transfer Gini coefficients sit below market Ginis is clear — but its effectiveness is CONDITIONAL and PARTIAL. It works best at moderate rates that raise revenue without provoking large disincentive or avoidance effects, and only when paired with well-designed transfers and long-run investment in education and health that tackle the causes of low income. Judged alone, and pushed to very high rates, its effectiveness is limited by avoidance, disincentives and its silence on wealth. So the most defensible conclusion is that progressive taxation is a NECESSARY but NOT SUFFICIENT component of an effective anti-inequality strategy.

---
**How this maps to our IB markband (Paper 1 part (b), out of 15):**

- **13–15:** the demands of the question are addressed, relevant economic theory is FULLY explained, relevant economic terms are used appropriately THROUGHOUT, where appropriate a diagram is included and FULLY explained, evaluation is EFFECTIVE and BALANCED, and a relevant real-world example is FULLY DEVELOPED.
- **10–12:** the demands of the question are addressed, relevant economic theory is explained, relevant economic terms are used appropriately mostly throughout, where appropriate a diagram is included and explained, evaluation is MOSTLY balanced, and a relevant real-world example is developed.
- **7–9:** the demands of the question are partially addressed, relevant economic theory is partly explained, some relevant economic terms are used, where appropriate a diagram is included, evaluation LACKS BALANCE, and a relevant real-world example is partly developed.
- **4–6:** relevant economic theory is described, evaluation is SUPERFICIAL, and a real-world example is identified.
- **1–3:** the answer is not relevant to the question and contains NO evaluation.

**Why this answer sits in the top band (13–15):**
- ✅ Demands addressed: it EVALUATES effectiveness, not merely describes progressive tax.
- ✅ Theory fully explained: the mechanism is derived (average tax rate → compressed disposable income → inward Lorenz shift → lower Gini), not just asserted.
- ✅ Terms used appropriately throughout: average tax rate, Gini coefficient, Lorenz curve, disposable income, equity–efficiency trade-off, vertical equity.
- ✅ Diagram included AND fully explained: the Lorenz diagram's axes, the 45° line and the inward shift are described and tied to the conclusion.
- ✅ Evaluation effective and BALANCED: genuine strengths AND limitations (disincentives, avoidance/evasion, root causes, wealth, need for complementary policies).
- ✅ Real-world example FULLY DEVELOPED: a multi-band income tax with post-tax-and-transfer Gini below market Gini, used to support the argument.
- ✅ Reasoned judgement: 'necessary but not sufficient', justified rather than tacked on.

Crucially, the top band is reached only because BOTH capping conditions are met: a FULLY DEVELOPED real-world example AND a genuinely BALANCED evaluation. An answer with the same theory but a vague 'for example, in some countries…' aside, or one that gave only the strengths of progressive tax, would drop to 7–9 ('lacks balance', 'example partly developed') no matter how well the theory was explained. Those two things — a developed example and two-sided evaluation — are what our marking engine looks for first.

sections.content: The Practice button marks YOUR own Paper 1 part (b) answer against this exact 15-mark band. When it returns a mark below the top band, the reason is almost always one of just two things: a missing or UNDEVELOPED real-world example (a passing mention is not enough — develop it), or ONE-SIDED evaluation (you argued the strengths of progressive tax but not the disincentive, avoidance and root-cause limitations, or gave no reasoned judgement). Fix those two and you move up the bands.
Key concepts in this lesson

This lesson develops three of the course's key concepts. **Equity** — the fairness of the income distribution, distinct from mere equality — frames the whole topic. **Intervention** — government use of taxes, transfers and public services to reshape market outcomes — is the toolkit. And **economic well-being** — living standards and the reduction of poverty — is the ultimate objective against which redistribution is judged. Keep all three in view: a strong part (b) answer links the mechanics of a policy back to its effect on equity and well-being, while weighing the efficiency cost of intervention.

sections.label: Get a Paper 1 (b) evaluation marked: is progressive taxation an effective way to reduce income inequality?
